29.64% ([[Nielsen ratings]]){{cite news|last=Braxton |first=Greg |title=Some Oscar Questions Linger: Like, Where Was Whitney? |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2000-mar-28-ca-13295-story.html |work=Los Angeles Times |access-date=June 27, 2013 |date=March 28, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131022224239/http://articles.latimes.com/2000/mar/28/entertainment/ca-13295 |archive-date=October 22, 2013 }} | last = 71st | next = 73rd }} The '''72nd Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), honored [[1999 in film|films released in 1999]] and took place on March 26, 2000, at the [[Shrine Auditorium]] in [[Los Angeles]], beginning at 5:30 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] / 8:30 p.m. [[Eastern Time Zone|EST]]. During the ceremony, the AMPAS presented [[Academy Awards]] (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 23 categories. The ceremony, televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], was produced by husband-and-wife producing team [[Richard D. Zanuck|Richard]] and [[Lili Fini Zanuck]] and was directed by Louis J. Horvitz. Actor [[Billy Crystal]] hosted the show for the seventh time. He first presided over the [[62nd Academy Awards|62nd ceremony]] held in 1990 and had last hosted the [[70th Academy Awards|70th ceremony]] held in 1998. Three weeks earlier in a ceremony at the [[Beverly Wilshire Hotel|Regent Beverly Wilshire Hotel]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]] held on March 4, the Academy Awards for Technical Achievement were presented by host [[Salma Hayek]].{{cite news|title=Oscar Watch: Hayek to present Scientific and Technical Awards |url=https://variety.com/2000/film/news/oscar-watch-154-1117776404/ |access-date=July 6, 2013 |work=Variety |publisher=Penske Media Corporation |date=February 13, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131014155527/http://variety.com/2000/film/news/oscar-watch-154-1117776404/ |archive-date=October 14, 2013 }} ''[[American Beauty (1999 film)|American Beauty]]'' won five awards, including [[Academy Award for Best Picture|Best Picture]].{{cite news|last=Lyman |first=Rick |title='American Beauty' Tops the Oscars; Main Acting Awards Go to Kevin Spacey and Hilary Swank |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2000/03/27/movies/american-beauty-tops-oscars-main-acting-awards-go-kevin-spacey-hilary-swank.html |date=March 27, 2000 |access-date=June 27, 2013 |work=The New York Times |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016233100/http://www.nytimes.com/2000/03/27/movies/american-beauty-tops-oscars-main-acting-awards-go-kevin-spacey-hilary-swank.html |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }}{{cite news|last=Brooks |first=Xan |title=Oscar ceremony sticks to the script |url=https://www.theguardian.com/culture/2000/mar/27/awardsandprizes.oscars20001 |access-date=July 6, 2013 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=March 27, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131014155740/http://www.theguardian.com/culture/2000/mar/27/awardsandprizes.oscars20001 |archive-date=October 14, 2013 }} Other winners included ''[[The Matrix]]'' with four awards, ''[[The Cider House Rules]]'' and ''[[Topsy-Turvy]]'' with two, and ''[[All About My Mother]]'', ''[[Boys Don't Cry (1999 film)|Boys Don't Cry]]'', ''[[Girl, Interrupted (film)|Girl, Interrupted]]'', ''[[King Gimp]]'', ''[[My Mother Dreams the Satan's Disciples in New York]]'', ''[[The Old Man and the Sea (1999 film)|The Old Man and the Sea]]'', ''[[One Day in September]]'', ''[[The Red Violin]]'', ''[[Sleepy Hollow (film)|Sleepy Hollow]]'', and ''[[Tarzan (1999 film)|Tarzan]]'' with one. The telecast garnered almost 47 million viewers in the United States. ==Winners and nominees== The nominees for the 72nd Academy Awards were announced on February 15, 2000, at 5:38 a.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] (13:38 [[Universal Time Coordinated|UTC]]) at the [[Samuel Goldwyn Theater]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]], by [[Robert Rehme]], president of the academy, and the actor [[Dustin Hoffman]].{{cite magazine|url=https://ew.com/article/2000/02/25/oscar-nominations-2000/ |title=And Then There Were 5 |first=Benjamin |last=Svetkey |access-date=May 6, 2013 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130603131232/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C275496%2C00.html |archive-date=June 3, 2013 }} ''[[American Beauty (1999 film)|American Beauty]]'' received the most nominations with eight total; ''[[The Cider House Rules]]'' and ''[[The Insider (film)|The Insider]]'' tied for second with seven nominations each.{{cite news|last=Ryan |first=Desmond |title=Eight Oscar Nominations For 'American Beauty' |url=http://articles.philly.com/2000-02-16/entertainment/25575844_1_oscar-nominations-cider-house-rules-supporting-actress-bid |access-date=July 6, 2013 |work=The Philadelphia Inquirer 5, 2013 }} The winners were announced during the awards ceremony on March 26, 2000.{{cite news|title=Oscar winners in full person to win Best Director for his directorial debut.{{cite news|last=Lyttelton |first=Oliver |title=Oscar Trivia: 50 Fun Facts To Prepare You For The 85th Academy Awards |url=http://blogs.indiewire.com/theplaylist/50-pieces-of-oscar-trivia-to-prepare-you-for-the-academy-awards-on-sunday-20130220?page=3 |access-date=July 5, 2013 |work=[[IndieWire]] |publisher=[[Snagfilms]] |date=February 20, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130330181810/http://blogs.indiewire.com/theplaylist/50-pieces-of-oscar-trivia-to-prepare-you-for-the-academy-awards-on-sunday-20130220?page=3 |archive-date=March 30, 2013 }} Best Actor winner [[Kevin Spacey]] became the tenth performer to win acting Oscars in both lead and supporting categories.{{cite web|url=http://www.oscars.org/awards/academyawards/legacy/ceremony/72nd.html |title=The 72nd Academy Awards (2000) |access-date=November 19, 2011 |work=Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences |publisher=AMPAS |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111107114701/http://www.oscars.org/awards/academyawards/legacy/ceremony/72nd.html |archive-date=November 7, 2011 }} By virtue of her father [[Jon Voight]]'s Best Lead Actor win for 1978's ''[[Coming Home (1978 film)|Coming Home]]'', Best Supporting Actress winner [[Angelina Jolie]] and Voight became the second Anderson III and Eric Allard |} ===Honorary Award=== * To [[Andrzej Wajda]] in recognition of five decades of extraordinary film direction.{{cite news|last=Hornaday |first=Ann |title=A 'Beauty' of an Evening |url=https://www.baltimoresun.com/2000/03/27/a-beauty-of-an-evening-awards-but-as-usual-the-oscar-telecast-dragged-on-endlessly-while-american-beauty-was-picking-up-its-five-awards-including-best-picture-best-actor-for-kevin-spacey-and-best-dire/ |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=[[The Baltimore Sun]] |publisher=Tribune Publishing |date=March 27, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131015120308/http://articles.baltimoresun.com/2000-03-27/features/0003270017_1_kevin-spacey-oscar-swank/2 |archive-date=October 15, 2013 }} ===Irving G. scope="col" width="55" | Nominations ! scope="col" align="center" | Film |- | style="text-align: center;" | 8 | ''[[American Beauty (1999 film)|American Beauty]]'' |- | rowspan=2 style="text-align:center" | 7 | ''[[The Cider House Rules]]'' |- | ''[[The Insider (film)|The Insider]]'' |- | style="text-align: center;" | 6 | ''[[The Sixth Sense]]'' |- | style="text-align: center;" | 5 | ''[[The Talented Mr. Ripley (film)|The Talented Mr. Ripley]]'' |- |rowspan=3 style="text-align:center" | 4 | ''[[The Green Mile (film)|The Green Mile]]'' |- | ''[[The Matrix]]'' |- | ''[[Topsy-Turvy]]'' |- |rowspan=4 style="text-align:center" | 3 | ''[[Being John Malkovich]]'' |- |''[[Magnolia (film)|Magnolia]]'' |- |''[[Sleepy Hollow (film)|Sleepy Hollow]]'' |- | ''[[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace]]'' |- |rowspan=5 style="text-align:center" | 2 | ''[[Anna and the King]]'' |- | ''[[Boys Don't Cry (1999 film)|Boys Don't Cry]]'' |- | ''[[The End of the Affair (1999 film)|The End of the Affair]]'' |- | ''[[Music of the Heart]]'' |- | ''[[Sweet and Lowdown]]'' |} {{col-2-of-2}} The following four films received multiple awards: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;" |- ! scope="col" width="55" | Awards ! scope="col" align="center" | Film |- | style="text-align: center;" | 5 | ''[[American Beauty (1999 film)|American Beauty]]'' |- | style="text-align: center;" | 4 | ''[[The Matrix]]'' |- |rowspan=2 style="text-align:center" | 2 | ''[[The Cider House Rules]]'' |- | ''[[Topsy-Turvy]]'' |} {{col-end}} ==Presenters and performers== The following individuals presented awards or performed musical numbers.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=295}} ===Presenters (in order of appearance)=== {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! "[[Alfie (Burt Bacharach song)|Alfie]]" from ''[[Alfie (1966 film)|Alfie]]'' |} ==Ceremony information== [[File:Billy Crystal VF 2012 Shankbone.JPG|thumb|130px|right|[[Billy Crystal]] hosted the 72nd Academy Awards.|alt=Billy Crystal in 2012.]] In view of the new millennium, the academy sought to both shorten the telecast and give the ceremony a new look.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=279}}{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=224}} Husband{{ndash}}and{{ndash}}wife producers [[Richard D. Zanuck]] and Lili Fini Zanuck were recruited to oversee the production of the 2000 ceremony. AMPAS President Robert Rehme explained the decision to hire the Zanucks saying, "With this new producing team in place, I look forward to a whole new perspective." This marked the first occurrence that a woman was tapped for producing duties at the Oscars.{{cite news|last=Snow |first=Shauna |title=Arts And Entertainment Reports From The Times, News Services And The Nation's Press |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1999-dec-11-ca-42694-story.html |access-date=July 6, 2013 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=December 11, 1999 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131015153058/http://articles.latimes.com/1999/dec/11/entertainment/ca-42694 |archive-date=October 15, 2013 }} Despite Richard and Lili's promises to make changes to the ceremony, they hired actor and veteran Oscar host [[Billy Crystal]] to host the ceremony for the seventh time. Production of the ceremony was reported to be far more ambitious and extravagant than previous ceremonies.{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=225}} Art director Bob Keene designed an ambitiously technological stage design for the telecast that used a floor adorned with flashing lights and several 35 foot columns consisting of [[high-definition video]] monitors stacked atop each other. The columns were used to display images of previous Oscar appearances as presenters took the stage, nomination packages, and reaction shots of the acting nominees as the winner was being announced.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=301}} Because of serious technical challenges concerning movement, lighting, and overheating, Keene and his production design team tested the stage at [[The Prospect Studios|ABC Prospect Studios]] before installing it at the [[Shrine Auditorium]].{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=235}} Several other people were involved in the production of the ceremony. Actor [[Peter Coyote]], who served as announcer for the telecast, was often seen before commercial breaks live behind the stage.{{cite news|last=Ryan |first=Joal |title=Taps for Oscar Night Taps |url=http://www.eonline.com/news/39121/taps-for-oscar-night-taps |access-date=July 5, 2013 |work=[[E!]] |publisher=[[NBCUniversal]] |date=December 10, 1999 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131015154742/http://www.eonline.com/news/39121/taps-for-oscar-night-taps |archive-date=October 15, 2013 }} Musical directors [[Burt Bacharach]], [[Don Was]], and Rob Shrock composed a techno-pop soundtrack that substituted for a live orchestra during most of the ceremony.{{harvnb|Pond|2005|pp=238–239}} In addition, Bacharach rounded up musicians that included [[Garth Brooks]], [[Queen Latifah]], and [[Dionne Warwick]] to perform a medley of songs previously nominated for [[Academy Award for Best Original Song|Best Original Song]].{{cite news|last=Burlingame |first=Jon |title=The Sound of Change |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2000-mar-20-ca-10676-story.html |access-date=July 6, 2013 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=March 20, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131015155435/http://articles.latimes.com/2000/mar/20/entertainment/ca-10676 |archive-date=October 15, 2013 }} Choreographer [[Kenny Ortega]] supervised the "[[Blame Canada]]" musical number.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=285}} ===Box office performance of nominees=== At the time of the nominations announcement on February 15, the combined gross of the five Best Picture nominees was $521 million with an average of $104 million per film.{{cite news|title=1999 Academy Award Nominations and Winner for Best Picture |url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=1999&p=.htm |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=[[Box Office Mojo]] |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130809031613/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=1999&p=.htm |archive-date=August 9, 2013 }} ''The Sixth Sense'' was the highest earner among the Best Picture nominees with $278.4 million in domestic box office receipts. The film was followed by ''The Green Mile'' ($120.7 million), ''American Beauty'' ($74.7 million), ''The Cider House Rules'' ($20.7 million), and finally ''The Insider'' ($26.6 million). Of the top 50 grossing movies of the year, 37 nominations went to 11 films on the list.{{cite web|url=https://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2000-02-07&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=1999&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |title=1999 Domestic Grosses |work=Box Office Mojo |access-date=March 2, 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140310213946/http://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2000-02-07&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=1999&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |archive-date=March 10, 2014 }} Only ''The Sixth Sense'' (2nd), ''The Green Mile'' (13th), ''The Talented Mr. Ripley'' (26th), and ''American Beauty'' (27th) were nominated for directing, acting, screenwriting, or Best Picture. The other top 50 box office hits that earned the nominations were ''Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace'' (1st), ''Toy Story 2'' (3rd), ''The Matrix'' (5th), ''Tarzan'' (6th), ''The Mummy'' (8th), ''Stuart Little'' (11th), and ''Sleepy Hollow'' (20th). ===Missing paper ballots=== Nearly two weeks before Oscar voting was finished, AMPAS reported that 4,000 of the ballots mailed to Academy members were missing. The bags that carried the ballots were mislabeled as [[Mail#Types|third-class mail]].{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=231}} On March 6, 2000, 1,000 of the ballots were discovered at a [[United States Postal Service|US Postal Service]] regional distribution center in [[Bell, California]].{{cite news|last=Ryfle |first=Steve |title=Oscar Ballots: Lost... And Found! |url=http://www.hollywood.com/news/brief/312040/oscar-ballots-lost-and-found?page=all |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=[[Hollywood.com]] |date=March 19, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016012727/http://www.hollywood.com/news/brief/312040/oscar-ballots-lost-and-found?page=all |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }} In response to affected members, AMPAS sent replacement ballots sealed in yellow envelopes, and extended the voting deadline by two days to March 23.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=289}} ===Oscar statuettes theft=== On March 10, 2000, 55 Oscar statuettes were stolen from a [[Roadway Express]] loading dock in [[Bell, California]].{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2000/mar/17/oscars2000.awardsandprizes |title=Oscars stolen, but the show will go on |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=March 17, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131015084005/http://www.theguardian.com/film/2000/mar/17/oscars2000.awardsandprizes |archive-date=October 15, 2013 }} In the event the stolen awards were to be still missing during the festivities, AMPAS announced that [[R.S. Owens & Company]], the manufacturer of the awards would produce a new batch of the golden statuettes.{{cite news|last=Ryan |first=Joal |author2=Steven Ryfle |title=Oscar: Found in Trash! |url=http://www.hollywood.com/news/brief/312091/oscar-found-in-trash?page=all |access-date=July 25, 2013 |work=Hollywood.com |publisher=Hollywood.com, LLC |date=March 19, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016025924/http://www.hollywood.com/news/brief/312091/oscar-found-in-trash?page=all |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }} Nine days later, 52 of the stolen statuettes were discovered in a trash bin at a [[Food 4 Less]] supermarket located in the [[Koreatown, Los Angeles|Koreatown]] neighborhood of Los Angeles by a man named Willie Fulgear.{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=255}}{{cite news|last=Lyman |first=Rick |title=Stolen Oscars Discovered in a Trash Bin |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2000/03/21/us/stolen-oscars-discovered-in-a-trash-bin.html |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=The New York Times |date=March 21, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130603011726/http://www.nytimes.com/2000/03/21/us/stolen-oscars-discovered-in-a-trash-bin.html |archive-date=June 3, 2013 }} For the safe recovery of the stolen statuettes, Roadway Express rewarded Fulgear with $50,000, and the academy invited him and his son Allen to the ceremony.{{cite news|title=Man Who Rescued Oscars Receives $50,000 Reward |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2000-mar-24-me-12196-story.html |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=March 24, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131023025904/http://articles.latimes.com/2000/mar/24/local/me-12196 |archive-date=October 23, 2013 }} Two Roadway Express employees, truck driver Lawrence Ledent and dock worker Anthony Hart, were arrested for the theft of the Oscars. Both men pleaded no contest. Ledent served six months in prison and Hart received probation. A third man who was Mr. Fulgear's half-brother was initially charged with the crime, but police dropped those charges after Mr. Fulgear divulged that they were estranged from each other.{{cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2000-oct-17-me-37786-story.html |access-date=July 8, 2013 |newspaper=Los Angeles Times |title=3rd Man Charged in Oscar Theft |date=October 17, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131023025908/http://articles.latimes.com/2000/oct/17/local/me-37786 |archive-date=October 23, 2013 }} Three years later, one of three remaining missing Oscar statuettes was discovered during a drug bust at a mansion in Miami, Florida; the other two have yet to be found.{{cite news|title=September 10, 2013 |url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2003/06/14/agents-stumble-upon-missing-oscar-statuette/ |access-date=October 18, 2014 |work=[[Chicago Tribune]] |publisher=Tribune Publishing |date=June 14, 2003 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150606115337/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2003-06-14/news/0306140104_1_fbi-agents-fbi-spokeswoman-loading-dock |archive-date=June 6, 2015 }} ===Critical reviews=== The show received a positive reception from most media publications. Television critic Monica Collins of the ''[[Boston Herald]]'' praised producers Richard and Lili Fini Zanuck for overseeing a show that was "clean, snappy, high-gloss and very well produced." She also quipped that host Billy Crystal did not need to save the show this time because "everything seemed to come together.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=314}} ''[[The San Francisco Examiner]]''{{'s}} Wesley Morris wrote "the show was downright hip, more so than it's been in decades." He also gave high marks for the "techno-chic" production elements from the music and stage design.{{cite news|last=Morris |first=Wesley |title=An 'American' tale |url=http://www.sfgate.com/style/article/An-American-tale-3067796.php#page-2 |access-date=June 27, 2013 |work=[[The San Francisco Examiner]] |publisher=[[Hearst Corporation]] |date=March 27, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016055127/http://www.sfgate.com/style/article/An-American-tale-3067796.php |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }} Columnist Paul Brownfield of the ''[[Los Angeles Times]]'' raved that "the 72nd annual Academy Awards telecast was hipper than in years past, sleeker in look and edgier in tone." He added that Crystal was "the perfect antidote to the entire evening's self-serious posturing."{{cite news|last=Brownfield |first=Paul |title=Hipper, Sleeker but Longer |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2000-mar-27-ca-13067-story.html |access-date=July 2, 2013 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=March 27, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131023032600/http://articles.latimes.com/2000/mar/27/entertainment/ca-13067 |archive-date=October 23, 2013 }} Some media outlets were more critical of the show. John Carman of the ''[[San Francisco Chronicle]]'' lamented that despite being solid and tidy, "the show never quite managed the big surprises, sloppy excesses and emotional highs we hope to see."{{cite news|last=Carman |first=John |title=The Show – Onstage and Off / TV Oscarcast Was Efficient, Lacked Emotion |url=http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/The-Show-Onstage-and-Off-TV-Oscarcast-Was-3240642.php |access-date=July 2, 2013 |work=San Francisco Chronicle |publisher=Hearst Corporation |date=March 27, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016071339/http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/The-Show-Onstage-and-Off-TV-Oscarcast-Was-3240642.php |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }} ''[[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]]'' television critic [[Rob Owen (journalist)|Rob Owen]] criticized the uneven pacing of the ceremony writing that the telecast "started slowly – 20 minutes of Billy Crystal's spoofs and singing that weren't as funny as his past Oscar intros – and never got up to speed."{{cite news|last=Owen |first=Rob |title=Crystal just ho-hum in a really slow Oscars show |url=http://old.post-gazette.com/tv/20000327owen.asp |work=Pittsburgh Post-Gazette |publisher=Crain Communications |access-date=July 2, 2013 |date=March 27, 2000 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016082906/http://old.post-gazette.com/tv/20000327owen.asp |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }} Caryn James of ''[[The New York Times]]'' remarked that "the four-hour show turned into a zombie." She also stated that the telecast was bloated with too many tributes to Hollywood's past.{{cite news|last=James |first=Caryn |title=CRITIC'S NOTEBOOK; Growing Pains as the Oscars Play to Jaded Stargazers |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2000/03/28/movies/critic-s-notebook-growing-pains-as-the-oscars-play-to-jaded-stargazers.html |access-date=July 5, 2013 |work=The New York Times |date=March 28, 2000 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016072236/http://www.nytimes.com/2000/03/28/movies/critic-s-notebook-growing-pains-as-the-oscars-play-to-jaded-stargazers.html |archive-date=October 16, 2013 }} ===Ratings and reception=== The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 46.52 million viewers over its length, which was a 3% increase from the previous year's ceremony. An estimated 79.11 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards. The show also drew higher [[Nielsen ratings]] compared to the previous ceremony with 29.64% of households watching over a 48.32 share.{{cite news|title=Academy Awards ratings |work=Television Bureau of Advertising |url=http://www.tvb.org/media/file/Academy_Awards.pdf |access-date=June 27, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130515152338/http://www.tvb.org/media/file/Academy_Awards.pdf |archive-date=May 15, 2013 }} It also drew a higher 18{{ndash}}49 demo rating with a 19.86 rating over a 39.34 share among viewers in that demographic. In July 2000, the show received nine nominations at the [[52nd Primetime Emmy Awards]].{{cite news|title=Primetime Emmy Award database |url=http://www.emmys.com/shows/72nd-annual-academy-awards |access-date=July 6, 2013 |work=[[Academy of Television Arts and Sciences]] |publisher=ATAS |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130623165922/http://www.emmys.com/shows/72nd-annual-academy-awards |archive-date=June 23, 2013 }} Two months later, the ceremony won one of those nominations for Louis J. Horvitz's direction of the telecast. =="In Memoriam"== The annual "In Memoriam" tribute, presented by actor [[Edward Norton]], honored the following people.{{harvnb|Bona|2002|p=307}} {{div col|colwidth=22em}} *[[Sylvia Sidney]] - Actress *[[Jim Varney]] - Actor *[[Ernest Gold (composer)|Ernest Gold]] – Composer *[[Ruth Roman]] - Actress *[[Henry Jones (actor)|Henry Jones]] - Actor *[[Robert Bresson]] – Director *[[Desmond Llewelyn]] - Actor *[[Allan Carr]] – Producer *[[Mario Puzo]] – Screenwriter *[[Rory Calhoun]] - Actor *[[Frank Tarloff]] – Screenwriter *[[Marc Davis (animator)|Marc Davis]] – Animator *[[Hedy Lamarr]] - Actress *[[Victor Mature]] - Actor *[[Garson Kanin]] – Screenwriter *[[Roger Vadim]] – Producer/Director *[[Mabel King]] - Actress *[[Oliver Reed]] - Actor *[[Albert Whitlock]] – Special Effects *[[Ian Bannen]] - Actor *[[Abraham Polonsky]] – Screenwriter *[[Dirk Bogarde]] - Actor *[[Lila Kedrova]] - Actress *[[Edward Dmytryk]] – Director *[[Buddy Rogers (actor)|Charles 'Buddy' Rogers]] - Actor/musician *[[Madeline Kahn]] - Actress *[[George C.
